serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve can of sparkling cider is approximately 330 ml, equivalent to about 330 g, providing roughly 158 kcal and 31.7 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Sparkling Cider — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (cr_id D01020005)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo record D01020005 for Sparkling cider (any flavour). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported in the HPB record; set to -1 where not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — apple juice and fruit-based carbonated beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for generic sparkling cider in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with apple juice (GI ~41–44, University of Sydney) and sugar-sweetened carbonated fruit beverages (GI ~55, Atkinson et al. 2008). A value of 55 is used as a conservative estimate given the high sugar content (9.62 g/100 g) and absence of fibre. Per the standard GI classification scale (low <55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 falls in the low category. Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (55 × 9.85 × 3.30) / 100 ≈ 17.9, rounded to 18.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.62 g/100 g) in a liquid form with zero dietary fibre, triggering insulin-mediated sodium retention and elevated blood pressure risk independent of the beverage's low sodium level (26 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid sparkling cider and all sugar-sweetened carbonated beverages; the AHA recommends limiting added sugar to <25 g/day for women and <36 g/day for men, and a single 330 ml can of this beverage delivers approximately 31.7 g of sugar, exceeding the daily limit in one serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rapidly absorbed liquid sugar (9.62 g/100 g, zero dietary fibre) causes acute postprandial glucose spikes; estimated GI of 55 combined with a glycemic load of ~18 per 330 ml serving makes this beverage unsuitable for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid sparkling cider entirely; the high sugar load in liquid form is absorbed faster than equivalent solid-food carbohydrates, and no fibre is present to attenuate the glycaemic response — plain sparkling water is the recommended substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk; this beverage provides 9.62 g sugar/100 g with no protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate sugar-sweetened sparkling beverages from their diet, as habitual intake is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD mortality risk per the American Heart Association 2018 advisory on sugar-sweetened beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar load (9.62 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to RA joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sugar-sweetened sparkling ciders; the high sugar content drives inflammatory mediator production and the beverage provides no omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or other anti-inflammatory factors to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sparkling cider (any flavour) is a commercially produced, carbonated apple-based beverage typically made from apple juice concentrate, water, carbon dioxide, natural flavours, and added sugar. It is distinct from alcoholic hard cider and is marketed as a non-alcoholic, fruit-flavoured sparkling drink. The beverage is clear to pale golden in colour, with a sweet, fruity taste and effervescent mouthfeel. It is widely available in Singapore and Southeast Asia in single-serve cans (330 ml) and bottles. Per 100 g, it provides 48 kcal, 9.85 g carbohydrate (of which 9.62 g is sugar), 0.1 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 26 mg sodium, with zero dietary fibre. The high sugar content and absence of fibre make this a high-glycaemic-load beverage with an estimated GI of 55 (low category). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar-sweetened beverage profile. A single 330 ml can delivers approximately 31.7 g of sugar, exceeding the AHA daily added-sugar limit for women in one serving. It is gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal-suitable, but should be consumed only occasionally and in small quantities by the general population.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in sparkling cider?
Is sparkling cider the same as apple juice?
Is sparkling cider suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record D01020005 (confidence 0.95 per input metadata). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, clearly documented. All hard gates satisfied.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D01020005 — sparkling cider contains no fat by product formulation. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — no fibre is present in this carbonated fruit-based beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo D01020005 record and are not available in the Singapore HPB database for this generic sparkling cider entry as of 2026. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on apple juice and sugar-sweetened carbonated fruit beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for generic sparkling cider was found. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is correctly classified as 'low'. This record covers the generic category 'sparkling cider (any flavour)' and individual branded products may vary in sugar content and GI.
